topic: Tali asking about her mum — emotional conversation

turns.content: I need to talk something through if that is alright. It is about one of my kids.
Of course. Take your time. What's going on?

Tali, my niece. I raise her. She has been asking about her mum again. Not the usual questions. Deeper ones. Why did it have to happen. Why her mum and not someone else. She sat on the verandah last night and just started talking and I did not know what to say to any of it.
Those are the kinds of questions that don't really have answers, which makes them incredibly hard to respond to. How old is Tali?

She is 14. Old enough now to think about things differently. When she was small she just wanted to know what her mum looked like, what her voice sounded like. Now she wants to understand the why of it. And there is no why that makes sense.

At 14, kids start moving from concrete thinking to more abstract processing of loss. She's not just missing her mum anymore -- she's questioning fairness and meaning. That's a hard transition, but it's also a sign of healthy emotional development. What did you do when she started talking?
I sat with her. Did not say much. Let her talk. She was not looking at me, just looking out at the dark. I think she needed to put the words out into the air more than she needed me to answer them.

That sounds like exactly what she needed. Being present without rushing to fill the silence is one of the most powerful things you can do. Many people feel pressure to offer explanations or comfort, but sometimes just being the safe person who listens is enough. Did she seem relieved after talking, or was she still unsettled?

She went quiet after a while and then she leaned into me a bit. Did not say anything else. I think she felt better for having said it. But I have been turning it over since then. Wondering if I should have said more. Given her something.

The fact that she leaned into you afterwards tells you a lot. She felt held by your presence even without words. If she had needed more from you, she likely would have pressed for answers or pulled away. Leaning in is a sign of comfort. That said, it's understandable that you're second-guessing yourself. Grief conversations with kids you love are never straightforward.

No they are not. And I carry my own feelings about it too. Same loss. But I try to keep that steady around her. She needs djalkiri. Solid ground. If I show too much of what I feel she might start worrying about me and that is not her job.

That's a generous instinct, wanting to be her stable foundation. But there's a middle ground that might help both of you. You don't have to fall apart in front of her, but letting her know that you miss her mum too -- even just a simple acknowledgment -- can normalise her grief. It tells her she's not alone in the feeling. It doesn't have to be a big emotional moment. Just a sentence.
Something like I miss her too.

Exactly that. Four words, but they change the shape of the conversation. They move it from her grief to shared grief, which is easier to carry. It doesn't put your emotions on her to manage. It just lets her know the weight isn't only hers.

I think I can do that. Next time she brings it up. And she will bring it up again. These things come in waves with her. She will be fine for weeks and then something triggers it. A song, a smell, seeing someone else's mum at school pickup.

Grief in waves is very normal, especially for young people. The triggers can seem random but they're usually tied to sensory memory or moments where the absence becomes suddenly visible. The fact that she comes to you when the wave hits is a sign of deep trust. She knows you're the safe place.
I want to always be that for her. Even when I am proper tired and do not have the words. I just want her to know she can always come sit with me and it will be alright.

She already knows that. Her behaviour tells you so. Coming to the verandah, talking into the dark with you beside her, leaning in when the words run out -- she's showing you that she trusts you completely. You don't need perfect words every time. You just need to keep being there, which you're already doing.
Thank you. I have been carrying this around for a few days and it helps to say it out loud to someone who is not in the middle of it. I feel a bit lighter now.
I'm glad it helped to talk it through. You're navigating something genuinely difficult, and you're doing it with a lot of care. Tali is lucky to have you in her corner.
